items.correct: When a sentence or phrase runs over from one line of poetry to the next without a punctuated pause, creating flow or surprise across the line break.
items.misconception: Enjambment is when a poem has very long lines.
items.random_wrong: Enjambment is when a poem uses no punctuation at all.
items.why: Enjambment is about syntax crossing the line break, not line length or punctuation in general. Students miss that it is about the relationship between grammar and lineation.

items.correct: Self-aware metafiction, intertextuality, scepticism toward grand narratives, playfulness with form, and blurring of high/low culture.
items.misconception: Postmodernism just means literature written after modernism.
items.random_wrong: Postmodernism means writing about the future.
items.why: Postmodernism is a specific set of attitudes and techniques, not a date range. A novel written in 2024 may be entirely traditional and not postmodern in any meaningful sense.

items.correct: The claim that judging a text by what the author 'intended' is fallacious — the text's meaning lies in the work itself, not the author's stated or guessed intentions.
items.misconception: It means authors can never make mistakes when writing.
items.random_wrong: It means it is wrong to write fiction.
items.why: Students assume 'author intent' settles meaning. Wimsatt and Beardsley argued it doesn't — meaning is a public property of the text, not a private fact about the author.

items.q: A recurring image across an author's work is a?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Motif. “Theme alone” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which writer is associated with recurring dystopias?
items.why: Correct answer: Margaret Atwood. “Jane Austen” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was The Handmaid's Tale published?
items.why: The real figure is 1985. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
